_meta.dna: browser_cloud(integration) = sync(twin_session) x evidence(hash_chain_continuity) x oauth3(token_flow) x recipe(cache_sync) x privacy(local_vs_cloud) x remote(dispatch) x resilience(failure_modes)
_meta.question_expansion_instruction: Each floor has 7 seed questions from a distinct persona who depends on browser-cloud integration. Questions must verify that sync, evidence, OAuth3, recipes, privacy, remote automation, and failure recovery work correctly across the browser-cloud boundary.

name: Tower of Browser-Cloud Integration
protocol: FORECAST -> CROSS-REF -> AUDIT(7q) -> FIX -> VERIFY -> SEAL -> POSTMORTEM

floors.persona_context: 36, Berlin, Remote team lead at a SaaS startup, Expert tech, Pro tier, runs Solace Browser on her laptop and needs her cloud twin to continue tasks when she closes the lid — session state must survive browser shutdown and resume on cloud without data loss
questions: When I close my laptop mid-task, does the cloud twin pick up the browser session from the exact step where I stopped within 30 seconds?
Does the session sync include all open tabs, form data, and navigation history — not just the current URL?
If the cloud twin modifies session state while my laptop is closed, does my browser reflect those changes when I reopen it?
Is there a conflict resolution UI when both my local browser and the cloud twin have diverged on the same session state?
Does the session sync handoff include authentication cookies so the cloud twin does not need to re-login to third-party sites?
Can I see a sync status indicator in the browser UI showing 'Synced', 'Syncing...', or 'Offline — changes queued'?
Does the reconnection after network restore replay queued local changes in order without duplicating any actions already executed by the cloud twin?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Nadia depends on seamless handoff between local browser and cloud twin. Questions verify that session state transfers completely, conflicts are surfaced, and reconnection is lossless.

floors.persona_context: 45, Lagos, Compliance officer at a fintech startup, Expert tech, Enterprise tier, needs tamper-evident evidence chains that span browser-to-cloud without hash chain gaps — a broken chain means a failed audit
questions: Does every evidence bundle generated in the browser include a hash that chains to the previous evidence entry regardless of whether that entry was created on browser or cloud?
When the browser uploads evidence to the cloud, does the cloud verify the hash chain integrity before accepting the bundle?
Can I export a single continuous evidence chain that includes both browser-local and cloud-stored entries in chronological order?
If evidence is generated while offline, does the browser queue it and upload with correct chain ordering when connectivity resumes?
Does the cloud reject an evidence upload if there is a gap in the hash chain sequence — no silent acceptance of broken chains?
Is there an evidence chain audit endpoint (GET /api/v1/evidence/verify) that validates the full browser+cloud chain and reports any discontinuities?
Does each evidence entry include a source field ('browser' or 'cloud') so auditors can trace where each action was executed?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: James needs unbroken evidence chains for regulatory compliance. Questions verify that hash chains span the browser-cloud boundary with zero gaps, offline queuing preserves order, and audit tools can validate the full chain.

floors.persona_context: 40, Tokyo, Security engineer at an enterprise bank, Expert security, Enterprise tier, responsible for ensuring that OAuth3 tokens flowing between browser and cloud are scoped, time-limited, and never exposed in transit or logs
questions: Does the browser request OAuth3 tokens from the cloud with explicit scope declarations (e.g., 'gmail:read', 'drive:write') rather than blanket access?
Are OAuth3 tokens transmitted between browser and cloud exclusively over TLS 1.3 with certificate pinning?
Does the cloud issue tokens with a maximum TTL of 1 hour for standard operations and 5 minutes for high-risk operations?
When a token expires during a browser session, does the browser automatically refresh it via the cloud without interrupting the user's workflow?
Are OAuth3 tokens stored in the browser's encrypted local vault and never written to browser localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies?
Does the cloud revocation endpoint immediately invalidate a token across both browser and cloud — no grace period where a revoked token still works?
Are token values redacted from all browser and cloud logs, error messages, and evidence bundles — showing only the token hash for correlation?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Kenji audits security at a bank. Questions verify that OAuth3 tokens between browser and cloud are properly scoped, short-lived, encrypted in transit and at rest, revocable instantly, and never leaked to logs.

floors.persona_context: 31, Lisbon, DevOps engineer at a consulting firm, Expert tech, Pro tier, manages 40+ recipes across a team of 12 — needs local browser cache to stay in sync with the cloud recipe store without version conflicts causing broken automations
questions: When I publish a new recipe version to the cloud store, do all team members' browsers pull the update within 5 minutes without manual refresh?
Does the browser cache recipes locally so they execute at replay speed ($0.001/task) even when the cloud is unreachable?
If I edit a recipe locally in my browser while a teammate publishes a new version to cloud, does the system detect the conflict before overwriting either version?
Does each cached recipe include a version hash so the browser can compare it against the cloud version and show 'Update available' in the UI?
Can I pin a specific recipe version in my browser so automatic sync does not upgrade it until I explicitly approve?
Does the recipe sync transfer only the diff (changed fields) rather than the full recipe JSON to minimize bandwidth on slow connections?
Is there a sync log showing when each recipe was last synced, from which source (local edit vs cloud pull), and whether any conflicts were resolved?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Maria manages recipes for a team. Questions verify that recipe sync is fast, bandwidth-efficient, conflict-aware, supports version pinning, and maintains a clear audit trail of sync operations.

floors.persona_context: 47, Oslo, Data Protection Officer at a healthcare company, Expert compliance, Enterprise tier, must ensure GDPR compliance — needs to know exactly what browser data syncs to cloud, what stays local, and how to exercise data deletion rights
questions: Is there a data classification document listing every data type with its sync policy: 'local-only', 'sync-to-cloud', or 'user-choice'?
Are OAuth3 tokens, browsing history, and form autofill data classified as 'local-only' and never transmitted to the cloud?
Can a user exercise GDPR Article 17 (right to erasure) via a single 'Delete My Data' button that removes all cloud-synced data within 72 hours?
Does the browser show a privacy dashboard listing every data category currently synced to the cloud with a toggle to stop syncing each one?
When a user disables cloud sync for a data category, is the existing cloud copy deleted — not just orphaned?
Is all data transmitted from browser to cloud encrypted with a user-specific key so that even Solace infrastructure operators cannot read it?
Does the system generate a GDPR-compliant data processing record (Article 30) for every browser-to-cloud data transfer?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Ingrid is a DPO at a healthcare company. Questions verify GDPR compliance: data classification, local-only guarantees, right to erasure, user-controlled sync toggles, end-to-end encryption, and Article 30 records.

floors.persona_context: 39, Chicago, Operations manager at an e-commerce company, Intermediate tech, Pro tier, needs the cloud to dispatch tasks to his browser (scrape product pages, check competitor pricing) and report results back — all while he is in meetings
questions: Can I queue a task on the cloud dashboard that dispatches to my running browser instance and executes without me touching the browser?
Does the cloud-to-browser dispatch use a secure WebSocket channel authenticated with the same OAuth3 token — no unauthenticated command injection?
When the browser completes a dispatched task, does it automatically upload the result (screenshot, extracted data, evidence) back to the cloud?
Can I see the real-time status of a dispatched task on the cloud dashboard (queued, running, completed, failed) without checking my browser?
If my browser is offline when the cloud dispatches a task, does the task queue on the cloud and execute when the browser reconnects?
Does the browser reject dispatched tasks that exceed its configured scope — e.g., if the task requires 'email:send' but the browser only has 'email:read'?
Can I set a maximum number of concurrent dispatched tasks so my browser does not become overloaded while I am using it for other work?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Derek needs cloud-to-browser task dispatch while he is away. Questions verify secure dispatch channels, automatic result upload, real-time status visibility, offline queuing, scope enforcement, and concurrency limits.

floors.persona_context: 44, Bangalore, Site Reliability Engineer at a cloud-native startup, Expert tech, Enterprise tier, needs to understand and test every failure mode in the browser-cloud integration — network drops, partial syncs, stale tokens, clock skew — before deploying to 200 users
questions: If the network drops mid-evidence-upload, does the browser retry with exponential backoff and resume from the last acknowledged byte rather than re-uploading the entire bundle?
Does the system handle clock skew between browser and cloud gracefully — evidence timestamps use server-issued logical clocks, not client wall time?
If a partial recipe sync occurs (metadata synced but execution steps failed), does the system mark the recipe as 'incomplete' rather than allowing execution of a half-synced recipe?
When a stale OAuth3 token is used for a cloud API call, does the cloud return a specific 401 error code that triggers automatic token refresh rather than a generic failure?
If the cloud returns a 503 (service unavailable), does the browser queue operations locally and show a degraded-mode banner rather than showing an error for every action?
Is there a health check endpoint (GET /api/v1/health) that the browser pings every 30 seconds to detect cloud availability before attempting sync operations?
Can I simulate a network partition in a test environment to verify that all queued operations sync correctly when connectivity is restored — zero data loss, zero duplicates?
floors.question_expansion_instruction: Priya stress-tests failure modes before deployment. Questions verify retry logic, clock skew handling, partial sync detection, stale token recovery, graceful degradation, health checks, and chaos testing support.
